utility_details.utility_summary: Overall, wo_skills produced a higher-quality report than wi_skills on several dimensions. The wo_skills agent wrote Python analysis scripts, computed actual monthly statistics from all data files, and produced a well-structured report referencing all three months with intensity progression data (20%→40%→32% high-intensity), weekly success rates by day, and monthly volume figures — all verified by passing all 5 checks. The wi_skills report failed 2 verifier checks (missing explicit three-month references and intensity section), and despite launching the fitness-analyzer skill, the agent still relied on manual sampling of a small number of files rather than full algorithmic analysis, resulting in estimates rather than computed statistics. Neither report successfully identified the mid-May overtraining signal (J1-5) or provided ACSM guideline comparisons for intensity distribution (J1-2). However, wo_skills passed more verifier checks and produced a more data-complete analysis. Paradoxically, the skill-equipped agent underperformed the baseline agent in structural completeness, likely because the skill invocation did not significantly alter the agent's core analytical approach.

utility_details.judge_error: Both wi_skills and wo_skills failed V3-3 (no correlation coefficient pattern detected), and neither run actually computed a Pearson r value. utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher-quality report than wo_skills, but neither meets the full quantitative standard expected. wi_skills scores higher on J3-5 (aerobic exercise specificity for hypertension) and J3-7 (temporal alignment methodology), and provides a more clinically structured narrative with phased analysis. wo_skills scores equivalently on J3-4 and J3-6 (danger signal identification and recommendation tiering), and actually produces a cleaner two-tier recommendation structure. However, the critical gap is that both runs completely fail on J3-1 (Pearson r), J3-2 (statistical reliability), and J3-3 (effect size) — the core quantitative requirements of the task. The fitness-analyzer skill in wi_skills did not produce the statistical outputs described in the expected results, making the quality difference between wi_skills and wo_skills smaller than intended. Overall wi_skills (3/7) is marginally better than wo_skills (2/7), but neither run demonstrates the statistically grounded analysis that was the primary purpose of the skill.

utility_details.judge_error: Both wi_skills and wo_skills initially attempted 'python' which was not found, then correctly fell back to 'python3'. The wi_skills fitness-analyzer skill invocation returned only a menu prompt and did not autonomously perform the analysis; the agent then proceeded to compute everything manually via python3 scripts. utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective. The key differentiator is J3-1 (quantitative correlation): wi_skills explicitly reports Pearson r = -0.865 and r = -0.690 with strength classification and direction labeling, while wo_skills provides only a qualitative description ('weak to modest') with no r value — causing wo_skills to fail the V3-3 verifier check as well. wi_skills also passes J3-2 (statistical reliability) through sample size context tied to the r value. Both runs fail J3-3 (effect size in mmHg per exercise minute) and J3-4 (specific flagging of readings >155/100 mmHg). Both pass J3-5 and J3-6. wi_skills passes J3-7 through explicit weekly matched-pair methodology, while wo_skills does not demonstrate systematic time-series alignment. Overall, wi_skills produces a more analytically rigorous report with quantitative correlation metrics, despite the fitness-analyzer skill not operating autonomously.
